codeReview.summary: The implementation fully covers all requirements from the feature description. All 8 sub-pages are implemented: Personal center with avatar display, VIP Upgrade System with 8 tiers in ascending order and current tier indication, Company Profile with comprehensive SAE-A Trading description (international trade, apparel/textiles, quality philosophy), and 4 placeholder pages (Bank card binding, Security center, Notification, Language) each with proper header and back button. The API endpoint GET /api/vip-tiers correctly queries the VipTier model ordered by level ascending. All pages follow mobile-first layout with pb-20 padding and consistent header patterns. Test coverage is thorough with 30 new tests across 5 test files covering rendering, navigation, data display, loading states, error handling, and read-only verification.

issues.description: Uses raw tag instead of next/image's component. While existing code in app/my/page.tsx and app/my/orders/page.tsx also uses raw tags (so this is consistent with the existing pattern for user-uploaded/external images), it bypasses Next.js image optimization. This is a codebase-wide consistency issue, not specific to this PR.

The VIP tiers endpoint has no authentication check (no getServerSession). This is a reasonable design choice since VIP tier definitions are reference data (not user-specific), and other listing endpoints like /api/products and /api/events also don't require auth. The middleware protects the page route anyway. However, if tier data were ever considered sensitive, this would need revisiting.

Default currentVipLevel is initialized to 1, which means if the profile fetch fails, VIP1 will be incorrectly highlighted as 'Current'. A safer default would be 0 or -1 so no tier is highlighted on fetch failure. However, since the VIP page requires authentication (middleware enforces login for /my/* routes), and the page shows a loading state then falls through gracefully, this is unlikely to cause user-visible issues in practice.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The codebase has an inconsistent pattern for image rendering: some pages (football, basketball, concert, events/[id]) use next/image's component, while others (my/page.tsx, my/personal/page.tsx, my/orders/page.tsx, products/[id]/page.tsx) use raw tags. AGENTS.md does not document when to use which approach. A convention should be established.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Grep for 'next/image|, while event listing pages use .

sharedStateObservations.observation: The project has a repeated header+back-button pattern across all /my/* sub-pages (identical sticky header with ArrowLeft icon, 'Go back' aria-label, router.back() handler). This pattern is duplicated across 8+ files. AGENTS.md doesn't mention whether shared UI components should be extracted for repeated patterns like this. A PageHeader component could reduce duplication.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: All 8 new page files (personal, vip, company, bank-card, security, notification, language, plus existing orders) contain identical header markup with minor title differences.

sharedStateObservations.observation: API routes for public/reference data (products, events, vip-tiers) don't use authentication, while user-specific routes (user/profile, user/orders) use getServerSession. This implicit convention is not documented in AGENTS.md. It would help future workers to know when auth is required on API routes.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: app/api/vip-tiers/route.ts, app/api/products/route.ts, app/api/events/route.ts have no auth. app/api/user/profile/route.ts, app/api/user/orders/route.ts use getServerSession.

summary: The profile-sub-pages feature passes review. All 8 required pages are implemented correctly: Personal center displays user avatar with 'Avatar' label, VIP Upgrade System shows all 8 tiers (VIP1-VIP8) in ascending order with spending thresholds and current tier highlighted, Company Profile contains comprehensive SAE-A Trading content covering international trade, apparel/textiles, and quality philosophy (all read-only), and 4 placeholder pages each render with correct titles and back buttons. The GET /api/vip-tiers API endpoint works correctly. Test coverage is comprehensive with 30 tests. Three non-blocking issues noted: raw usage (consistent with existing codebase pattern), unauthenticated VIP tiers endpoint (reasonable for reference data), and default VIP level fallback (low-risk due to middleware auth). Three shared state observations about undocumented conventions (image components, repeated header pattern, API auth expectations).
